beats.title: Five ways to keep meat — no power
items: Salt curing (the $5 box of salt)
The smokehouse
The cool cellar
Potting in fat (confit)
Drying (jerky)

beats.caption: every cut is still spoilable — only a steady feed of electricity holds it at the edge of the cliff

beats.title: The whole secret
items: Rot needs tiny living things — bacteria
Bacteria must have water to live
Cold only slows them (never kills)
The old ways take the water away

layers.label: Salty dry rind — no water left
Rosy cured meat — good for a year

beats.title: Two ways to salt
items: DRY: bury the cut in a salt box
Re-salt partway, pour off the water
BRINE: salt water that floats an egg
Sugar + spices = corned beef, hams

beats.label: of curing — kept cold, just above freezing, the old butcher's rule

beats.title: Use real curing salt (pink, nitrite)
beats.desc: For anything smoked or aged, plain salt is NOT enough. The nitrite guards against botulism — invisible, no smell, no taste. Follow the package.

beats.title: Cold smoke vs hot smoke
items: COLD: cool, preserves, does NOT cook
→ needs the cure + curing salt first
HOT: warm, cooks as it smokes
→ for sausage & fish you eat soon

beats.caption: the cellar isn't preserving — the salt and smoke already did. It's just where a year of meat hangs and waits

beats.title: A whole plain system
items: Salt — pulls the water, stops the rot
Smoke — seals the surface
Cellar — where it hangs & waits
Fat & dry air — the rest

items.text: Curing salt for smoked/aged — follow the package
Plenty of salt — under-salting is the #1 failure
Smells foul or slimy? Throw it out

beats.title: Too little salt, or too much warmth
beats.desc: Both leave water for the rot to take hold. Use the full salt the old recipes call for, and cure in the cold. That's the whole of it.
